nativeRange.habitat: Reef-associated species commonly found around outer reef slopes, lagoons, drop-offs, and coral heads; tends to shelter under overhangs and inside caves during the day and emerge to forage at night.

diet.notes: In the wild has been reported to feed mainly on benthic crabs, shrimps, and other small crustaceans, hunting at night. In captivity generally accepts a varied meaty diet of mysis, enriched brine shrimp, chopped marine meats, krill, and frozen carnivore preparations; newly introduced individuals sometimes need live saltwater feeder shrimp to initiate feeding. Two small feedings per day are commonly advised.

avoidWith: small fish that fit in its mouth

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by J. Hutsch, 3264x2448. Vivid bright-red individual in situ (Red Sea); clearly shows both diagnostics - rows of white-spotted scales and the silvery-white blotch on the caudal peduncle - sharp and well-lit. Minor foreground anthias but subject dominant.
Photo by Diego Delso, 1667x1111. Clean full-body side profile of a vibrant uniform-red specimen with large eye; correct ID (no lateral stripes), sharp, good composition. Different angle for variety.
Photo by Bernard DUPONT, 3234x1940. Front three-quarter view of a bright-red individual; large eye and uniform red body consistent with the species. Adds a frontal angle.

summary: The Squirrelfish (Sargocentron caudimaculatum) is a nocturnal Indo-Pacific reef fish reaching around 25 cm, recognized by its bright red body and a distinctive white blotch at the base of its tail. It is considered broadly hardy but tends to prey on small fish and motile invertebrates.
careNotes: The Tailspot Squirrelfish is a strictly nocturnal species that generally spends the daylight hours sheltering under overhangs and inside caves, emerging at dusk to forage. It tends to do best in aquariums with ample rockwork providing multiple shaded retreats, dimmed or actinic-only lighting during the day, and open swimming space for its active nighttime movement. A tightly-sealed lid is generally advised, as squirrelfishes have been known to startle and jump. Handling caution is warranted: Sargocentron species carry sharp, reportedly venomous preopercular and opercular spines (the bony projections along the gill covers), and their rough scales easily snag in netting — a container is generally preferred over a net when moving the fish. Considered reef-safe with caution; sessile corals are generally left undisturbed, but the species has been reported to consume small fish, ornamental shrimp, small crabs, and other small motile invertebrates that can fit in its relatively large mouth. Most keepers house it singly; conspecifics or other close relatives can occasionally be kept together when introduced simultaneously to a sufficiently large aquarium with multiple hiding spots, but this is not commonly attempted in home systems. The Squirrelfish belongs to the subfamily Holocentrinae, which is closely related to the soldierfishes (Myripristinae) and is broadly distinguished by its sharper, more pronounced opercular spines. Adults can reach around 25 cm, though a more typical size in the aquarium is closer to 18 cm.

primary.name: Reefapp.net
primary.notes: Designated primary (LiveAquaria) has no current product page for Sargocentron caudimaculatum (homepage 200, candidate product slug 404). Substituted Reefapp.net as primary — provides aquarium-specific care guidance: adult size 25 cm, minimum tank ~800 L, temperament 'might be aggressive', reef-safe with caution toward invertebrates, temperature ~26 °C, SG ~1.026, nocturnal habit, and prey list (small crustaceans, krill, mysis, artemia).

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (family Holocentridae, order Holocentriformes), distribution (Red Sea and East Africa east to French Polynesia, north to Japan, south to Australia), depth range 2–40 m, max TL 25 cm (common ~18 cm), wild temperature 25–29 °C (mean 27.9 °C), nocturnal habit, and diet of benthic crabs and shrimps.

additional.name: Wikipedia
additional.notes: Confirmed common names (Silverspot/Whitetail Squirrelfish), taxonomy, max size 25 cm TL, depth range 2–40 m, native distribution Indian and Pacific Oceans, nocturnal predator on crabs and shrimp, IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: LiveAquaria (Sammara Squirrelfish reference page)
additional.notes: Designated primary LiveAquaria has no species page for Sargocentron caudimaculatum, so this congeneric reference page (Neoniphon sammara) was used to cross-check hobby water parameters (72–78 °F → 22–26 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025), reef-safe-with-caution status, carnivore diet, and nocturnal squirrelfish husbandry conventions.

additional.name: Reefguide.org
additional.notes: Reef-fish identification reference confirming Tailspot/Silverspot common names and Indo-Pacific reef habitat for Sargocentron caudimaculatum.
